class_schedule.topic: What Is Education? Historical and Philosophical Foundations
learning_objectives: Define education in historical and contemporary contexts
Identify major philosophical traditions in education
Distinguish between formal, informal, and non-formal education
readings: Dewey, J. - Democracy and Education, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Education is one of humanity's oldest and most transformative institutions. This session surveys its evolution from ancient Greece through the modern era.
key_concepts: The role of schooling in democratic societies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline activity mapping major educational movements to historical events

review.value: Perennialism, essentialism, progressivism, and social reconstructionism each define the purpose of education differently
Education is not preparation for life but life itself; learning through experience and reflection

learning_objectives: Describe the organizational structure of K-12 and higher education in the U.S.
Explain the roles of federal, state, and local governance in education
Compare public, private, and charter school models
readings: National Center for Education Statistics overview reports

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding how educational systems are organized is essential for anyone entering the field. We examine governance, funding, and accountability structures.
key_concepts: Federal vs. state vs. local control of education
School districts and boards of education
Public, private, charter, and magnet schools
Higher education structures: community colleges, universities, for-profit institutions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Organizational chart exercise mapping a local school district's governance structure
setup_requirements: Access to local school district website

review.value: The U.S. Constitution leaves education to the states, but federal policy (e.g., ESSA) shapes practice through funding conditions
Charter schools operate with public funding but independent governance; voucher programs redirect public funds to private schools

learning_objectives: Define curriculum and distinguish between explicit, implicit, and null curricula
Explain Tyler's rationale for curriculum design
readings: Tyler, R. - Basic Principles of Curriculum and Instruction (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Curriculum is more than a list of topics. It embodies values, priorities, and assumptions about what knowledge matters and who should have access to it.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a sample state curriculum standard and trace it to a classroom lesson plan
setup_requirements: Sample curriculum documents from state department of education

review.value: Four questions: What purposes? What experiences? How organized? How evaluated?
Unspoken lessons about power, conformity, and social norms transmitted through school routines and structures

class_schedule.topic: Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion in Education
learning_objectives: Identify systemic barriers to educational equity
Explain the achievement gap and opportunity gap frameworks

lecture_notes.introduction: Equitable education requires understanding how race, class, gender, language, and ability shape students' experiences and outcomes.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of a school district's equity audit and action plan
setup_requirements: Access to NCES data tools for demographic analysis

review.value: Disparities in access to resources, qualified teachers, advanced coursework, and safe learning environments
Using students' cultural references as assets in the learning process, not deficits to overcome

learning_objectives: Trace major education policy milestones from Brown v. Board to ESSA
Evaluate the impact of standardized testing on instruction
Analyze current policy debates in education
readings: Ravitch, D. - The Death and Life of the Great American School System (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Education policy shapes what happens in classrooms. Understanding the political landscape is essential for educators who want to advocate for change.
key_concepts: Brown v. Board of Education and desegregation
A Nation at Risk and the standards movement
No Child Left Behind and high-stakes testing
Race to the Top and accountability
Every Student Succeeds Act and state flexibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy timeline jigsaw activity: each group presents a key policy era to the class
setup_requirements: Access to federal education legislation texts

review.value: A Nation at Risk (1983) catalyzed standards-based reform; NCLB (2001) mandated annual testing and consequences
Returned significant authority to states while maintaining equity provisions and accountability requirements

class_schedule.topic: The Teaching Profession and Professional Ethics
learning_objectives: Describe pathways into the teaching profession
Explain professional standards and ethical codes for educators
Reflect on the role of the educator in society
readings: NEA Code of Ethics for Educators

lecture_notes.introduction: Teaching is both an art and a profession. This session examines what it means to be a professional educator and the ethical responsibilities that come with the role.
key_concepts: Teacher preparation: traditional vs. alternative pathways
Ethical obligations to students, families, and communities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ethical dilemma scenarios: small group discussion and class debrief
setup_requirements: Access to NEA Code of Ethics document

review.value: InTASC standards define what effective teaching looks like across ten categories from content knowledge to professional responsibility
Educators have fiduciary responsibilities to students; ethical codes guide decisions about fairness, privacy, and professional boundaries

questions.question: Compare and contrast essentialism and progressivism as educational philosophies. How does each define the purpose of education and the role of the teacher?
questions.answer: Essentialism holds that schools should transmit a core body of knowledge and skills, with the teacher as an authority figure directing instruction. Progressivism, influenced by Dewey, argues that education should center on student interests and real-world problem-solving, with the teacher as a facilitator of inquiry. Essentialism emphasizes discipline and rigor; progressivism emphasizes experience and democracy.

questions.question: Explain the concept of the 'null curriculum' and provide an example of how something excluded from the curriculum can affect students.
questions.answer: The null curriculum refers to what schools choose NOT to teach. By excluding certain topics, schools send implicit messages about what knowledge is (or isn't) valued. For example, omitting personal finance from the high school curriculum means students graduate without understanding budgeting, credit, or investing, which disproportionately affects students from low-income families who lack alternative sources for this knowledge.

quizzes.topic: Equity, Policy, and the Teaching Profession

questions.question: Distinguish between the 'achievement gap' and the 'opportunity gap.' Why does the framing matter for policy and practice?
questions.answer: The achievement gap refers to disparities in test scores and academic outcomes between demographic groups. The opportunity gap reframes this by pointing to unequal access to resources, experienced teachers, advanced courses, and safe environments. The framing matters because 'achievement gap' can imply student or cultural deficits, while 'opportunity gap' directs attention to systemic inequities that policy can address.

rubric.key: Connects to policy or practice implications

questions.question: How did No Child Left Behind (NCLB) change the relationship between the federal government and local schools? Name one positive and one negative consequence.
questions.answer: NCLB required annual standardized testing in grades 3-8 and tied school funding to adequate yearly progress (AYP), dramatically expanding federal influence over local education. Positive: it required disaggregated data by race, income, and disability, making achievement gaps visible and actionable. Negative: it incentivized teaching to the test, narrowing the curriculum and reducing time for science, social studies, and the arts.

major_assignments.title: Educational Philosophy Position Paper
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,000-word paper articulating your personal philosophy of education. Draw on at least three philosophical traditions discussed in class, situate your philosophy in relation to current educational debates, and explain how your philosophy would guide your decisions as an educator or educational technologist.
objectives: Synthesize multiple philosophical traditions into a coherent personal stance
Connect abstract philosophy to concrete educational practice
Demonstrate scholarly writing with proper citations
Reflect critically on assumptions about the purpose of education

criteria: Accurately represents at least three philosophical traditions
Demonstrates understanding of nuances and tensions between philosophies
Articulates a clear personal stance with justification
Connects philosophy to specific educational decisions
Shows awareness of how philosophy shapes technology use in education
Clear, organized prose with logical flow
Free of grammatical and mechanical errors
Thoughtful, well-argued personal philosophy of education
Clear connection between philosophy and educational practice

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes assessing comprehension of readings and key concepts

participation.description: Active engagement in discussions, case study analyses, and in-class activities

major_assignments.description: Philosophy position paper and analytical writing

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering educational history, philosophy, and structure

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam integrating all course themes with emphasis on policy and professional ethics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction. Participation requires presence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 5% per day late, up to 5 days. After 5 days, no credit is given without documented extenuating circumstances.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and study groups are encouraged. All written work must be your own. Cite all sources using APA 7th edition.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, fabrication, or submitting another's work as your own results in a failing grade for the assignment and referral to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to a computer with internet for research and course management system. No specialized software required for this course.
